# Workstream State Model — Three-Axis Design

Status: **agreed intent** (design session, grill-me). This document pins the
intended state model for T3 Code Workstream threads. A separate implementation
plan and coder follow; this is the contract they build to.

Supersedes the single-`status` model in `.plans/phase-c-status-and-dependencies.md`
(effective-display precedence, permissive transitions) and refines the dispatcher
contract in `.plans/phase-d-core-dispatcher-design.md`.

---

## 1. Problem — one field, three meanings

Today a single stored `ThreadStatus` (`planned | running | blocked | review |
done | error`) conflates three orthogonal things, which is the root cause of the
observed misuse:

- **Plan intent** — where a node sits in the intended design (a lane an
  agent/human chooses).
- **Runtime condition** — whether the agent loop is literally executing a turn
  right now.
- **Attention** — whether a human is needed, and why.

Because they share one writable enum, they desync and get misused:

- `review` is told to a `coder` whose output flows to a separate `reviewer`
  thread — conflating "a *reviewer thread* will process this" (automatic) with
  "a *human* must review this" (pause). `review` was never defined for the
  thread, so it was used meaninglessly.
- `blocked` conflates "waiting on upstream work" (resolves itself) with "paused
  awaiting a human" (won't resolve without action).
- A node can read `running` while nothing executes, or execute while labelled
  otherwise — "describes the plan" and "is the truth" silently disagree.
- A re-engaged `done` node (a follow-up question beyond original scope) has no
  way to show *activity* without appearing to revert to incomplete.

The runtime overlay already half-exists on the read path
(`resolveBaseColumn`/`getEffectiveColumn` derive `running` from live
session/turn signals; `getEffectiveColumn` derives `blocked` from unmet
dependencies). The bug is that these derived facts share one vocabulary with —
and are overwritten by — the agent-writable stored field.

---

## 2. The model — three independent axes

State is decomposed into **three axes**. The slogan *"status describes the plan;
runtime is the truth"* becomes literally true because an agent can only ever
write plan/attention intent, never the runtime or the control-plane-owned
values.

### Axis 1 — Plan lane (intent; the kanban board)

Mutually-exclusive waypoints describing the node's intended position in the
design. This is the only "lifecycle" axis and it is deliberately small:

```
planned  →  ready  →  in_progress  →  done
                                  ↘  cancelled   (terminal, from any pre-terminal lane)
```

| lane | meaning |
|---|---|
| `planned` | in the plan, **staged/held** — not released to run |
| `ready` | **released** — will run once dependencies clear |
| `in_progress` | the active phase of the plan is underway |
| `done` | accepted-complete — **releases dependents** |
| `cancelled` | abandoned (was planned, deliberately stopped) — terminal, does **not** release dependents |

Everything that used to muddy the lane moves off it:

| old `status` value | where it lives now |
|---|---|
| `planned` / `done` | plan lane (unchanged) |
| `running` | split: plan lane `in_progress` (control-plane-set) + runtime `executing` (derived) |
| `blocked` (on upstream) | **derived** from unmet dependencies — never stored |
| `review` | **attention** reason = *awaiting acceptance* (plan stays `in_progress`) |
| `blocked` (paused on a human) | **attention** reason = *needs guidance* |
| `error` | **attention** reason = *failed/stalled* (system-set) |
| pending approval / user-input | **attention** reasons (already derived today) |

### Axis 2 — Runtime / activity (the truth; derived, never stored as intent)

A pure projection over the session and latest turn — *is a turn turning right
now?* Never an agent-writable field.

| runtime | derivation |
|---|---|
| `executing` | `session.status === "running"` or `latestTurn.state === "running"` |
| `starting` | session connecting / spinning up (transient) |
| `idle` | session exists, no active turn |
| (none) | no session yet |

Activity **bubbles up**: a subtree with any `executing` descendant reads as
active *regardless of plan state*. A re-engaged `done` node is `done` (plan) +
`executing` (activity) at once.

The only stored "activity-ish" value is the plan lane `in_progress`, whose sole
writer is the control plane at kickoff. There is nothing to keep in sync, so it
cannot desync.

### Axis 3 — Attention (needs-a-human; the single notification surface)

A reason-tagged flag that can co-exist with any plan lane and bubbles up. This is
the **one** axis the notification/escalation machinery watches.

| attention reason | raised by |
|---|---|
| `error` (failed / stalled / looping) | **system** (liveness sweep) |
| `awaiting_approval` | **system** (open approval request) |
| `awaiting_input` | **system** (open user-input request) |
| `awaiting_acceptance` (review/sign-off) | **agent** ("my output needs sign-off") |
| `needs_guidance` (stuck) | **agent** ("I can't proceed without a human") |

Attention is a union of **derived** reasons (error, approval, input) and
**agent-raised** reasons (acceptance, guidance). When the underlying condition
clears (the human answers, the plan advances to `done`/`cancelled`, the agent
resumes), the flag clears.

> Names above are the proposed wire contract. Australian-English prose is used
> throughout this document; identifiers stay conventional.

---

## 3. Two orthogonal start-gates

A thread starts executing only when **both** gates clear — and the two gates are
the same "waiting on work vs waiting on a human" split, applied at the launch
boundary:

1. **Dependency gate** (automatic, structural): every `blockedBy` thread is
   `done`. Waiting here ⇒ board shows **blocked (on upstream)**; resolves itself.
2. **Release gate** (intentional): plan lane is `ready`, not `planned`. Waiting
   here ⇒ the plan is **staged but unreleased**; needs a human/agent to release.

The dispatcher promotes when `lane == ready && deps satisfied && not started`
(today's trigger is just deps-satisfied — this adds the release gate).

`planned` is therefore a genuine **hold**: even with deps clear it sits still
until released. This enables laying out a whole DAG for human review of the work
breakdown *before any tokens are spent*.

**Release semantics:** releasing flips the whole subtree `planned → ready` at
once; dependency edges still sequence execution layer by layer.

**Spawn default:** a plain `workstream_spawn` defaults to `ready` (current
"spawn → it runs" ergonomics preserved). **Staging is the explicit opt-in**
(spawn `planned`), used for the review-the-graph flow.

---

## 4. Start / stop / re-engage

### Start ("execution drives status")

The dispatcher remains the **sole start authority**. On kickoff it sends the
first-turn prompt **and** sets plan lane → `in_progress` in one atomic
transaction (today's `setRunning`, retargeted). Runtime → `executing` follows
from the turn running. The act of starting writes `in_progress`; an agent never
does.

### `done` is sticky (activity ≠ plan)

A started turn pulls a **pre-completion** lane (`ready`, or `in_progress` with a
raised attention flag) to `in_progress`. It does **not** revert a terminal lane:

- Re-engaging a `done` node (out-of-scope follow-up) lights the **activity** axis
  only; the lane stays `done`. Reopening the plan requires an *explicit* lane
  change — implicit un-completing is the bug being removed.
- Answering an `awaiting_acceptance` thread (sending revision feedback) resumes
  it → `in_progress`; accepting it → `done`.

### Stop, and the "no silent halt" invariant

There is a mechanic to **stop** a running agent (`thread.turn.interrupt`
interrupts the active turn; runtime → `idle`). Restarting is typically just
sending a prompt (which progresses the next turn → `executing`).

**Invariant:** *a node that is not executing, will not be auto-resumed, and is
not plan-terminal (`done`/`cancelled`) must carry a raised attention flag.*
Nothing that needs a human may sit silently halted. The discriminator is "is
there an owner who will resume it?" — the same resolves-itself-vs-needs-a-human
split.

Enforcement is **both** (decision: option c):

- **Synchronous, where intent is known.** A **human** stop with no auto-resumer
  defaults to raising `needs_guidance` (so it surfaces immediately). An
  **orchestrator** pause-to-rethink (it owns the restart) leaves the lane
  `in_progress` and raises nothing.
- **Asynchronous backstop.** The existing idle / "forgot to finish" rail catches
  anything that ends up `in_progress` + `idle` + no resume pending (a dropped
  orchestrator, a crash mid-pause) and surfaces/wakes after its grace window.

> The idle-rail's false-positive fix is a **separate handoff**; this design only
> relies on the rail as the backstop and does not change it.

---

## 5. Graph rollups — three projections, not one fused state

The subtree rollup (`rollupGraphState`) must carry **separate** projections so a
graph can be e.g. "plan: done" and "activity: active" simultaneously (the
re-engaged-`done` case):

- **Plan rollup** — is all intended work `done` (vs in-progress / cancelled)?
- **Activity rollup** — is anything `executing`?
- **Attention rollup** — does anything need a human, and the highest-priority
  reason (the existing `attentionReasonOf` priority ladder)?

Today these are fused ("active dominates"), which is the source of the
node-vs-graph confusion.

---

## 6. Load-bearing vs descriptive

**Load-bearing** (a few, machine-checked — must be strict):

- `done` (plan) — the *only* thing that releases dependents and lets the
  dispatcher promote the next thread.
- the `blockedBy` dependency gate + the `ready`/`planned` release gate — the two
  start-gates.
- attention `error` and the agent-raised reasons — drive notification / parent
  wakes (the attention axis is the notification surface).
- terminal-ness for the generation join barrier: `done`, `cancelled`, and any
  attention-flagged-and-idle node ("won't progress without a human").

**Descriptive** (most of it — generous, human-facing):

- `planned` vs `ready` distinction beyond the release gate, `in_progress` as a
  label, the runtime axis, and the board projections (`blocked`, `ready`,
  "working between turns"). These describe; they don't gate.

---

## 7. Tool surface (agent-facing)

The agent no longer sets one flat enum mixing plan and attention. It does two
conceptually distinct things:

- **Advance the plan** — small set: `done`, `cancel`, and `ready`/`planned` for
  staging. (`in_progress` is never agent-settable.)
- **Raise attention** — with a reason: `awaiting_acceptance` (review) or
  `needs_guidance` (stuck).

The child kick-off prompt's guidance ("`done` when finished, `review` if your
output must be reviewed, `blocked` if you cannot proceed") maps to: `done` =
advance plan; `review` = raise `awaiting_acceptance`; `blocked` = raise
`needs_guidance`. Prompt text stays principle-based, not absolute directives.

---

## 8. Authorisation

Extends the existing "own thread / own direct children" precedent:

| write | allowed actor |
|---|---|
| plan `ready` / `planned` (staging), `done`, `cancel` | the thread itself, or its **direct parent** |
| raise attention `awaiting_acceptance` / `needs_guidance` | the thread itself (or its direct parent) |
| plan `in_progress` | **control plane only** (set by starting) |
| attention `error` | **system only** (liveness) |
| attention `awaiting_approval` / `awaiting_input` | **system only** (derived from open requests) |
| start / stop / release-subtree | the thread's **direct parent** orchestrator, or a human |

Agents drive *plan + agent-raised attention* on themselves and their direct
children; the system exclusively owns `in_progress`, `error`, and the derived
attention reasons. The decider remains the single chokepoint that enforces these
(as it already does for the server-only `error` guard).

---

## 9. Migration — clean break, no compat shim

Prototype: a **breaking change** with a one-time best-effort remap, no
dual-shape coexistence, no "kept for backward compatibility" fields.

Best-effort remap of persisted threads:

| old `status` | new state |
|---|---|
| `planned` | plan `planned` (note: new default is `ready` for fresh spawns; see §3) |
| `running` | plan `in_progress` (runtime re-derives `executing`/`idle`) |
| `blocked` | derive: unmet deps → board-blocked (no stored value); else attention `needs_guidance` |
| `review` | attention `awaiting_acceptance`, plan `in_progress` |
| `done` | plan `done` |
| `error` | attention `error` |

Oddities in old/in-flight threads during transition are acceptable.

---

## 10. Open questions (deferred deliberately)

- **Pending *automated* review marker.** Dropped for now. "Awaiting automated
  review" is already visible on the board as the **reviewer thread's own
  activity** (the upstream node is `done`; the reviewer node is live). Re-add a
  dedicated upstream marker only if a concrete need arises.
- **Stored `ready` under a concurrency cap.** `ready` is stored now to serve the
  staging/hold use case. If a concurrency cap is later added, `ready` also gains
  a queue-backpressure meaning ("eligible, awaiting a start slot") — no model
  change anticipated, just richer board semantics.
- **Final attention vocabulary / UI surfacing** of reasons (single badge vs
  per-reason) — settle during implementation against the existing
  `attentionReasonOf` ladder.
</content>
</invoke>

content.thinking:
I'm seeing how the three-axis model maps onto my liveness redesign, particularly how State B (waiting for input) corresponds to the attention reasons like `awaiting_input` and `awaiting_approval` that are system-set and derived from open requests. This makes those states first-class in the sweep logic, which already skips threads with active attention items, so my Phase 1 State B guard is partially covered but I should align the new sweep skip behavior.

For State C (recoverable stall), the design explicitly distinguishes between situations that won't resolve without human intervention and provides `needs_guidance` for those cases, with the "no silent halt" invariant and idle-rail backstop as safety nets. The key tension I'm working through is whether a recoverable stall should raise `needs_guidance` (recoverable, surfaces to human) or `error` (failed/stalled/looping) — the current design maps stall to `error`, but the user's concern about distinguishing failed from stalled suggests stall should be recoverable and re-promptable rather than terminal.

However, the redesign already substantially softens this: attention=`error` no longer terminally kills dependents or changes the plan lane like the old status.set error did. The thread stays in_progress and the runtime keeps going. So the real Phase 3 value is distinguishing genuine failure from recoverable stall (via `needs_guidance` or auto-nudge), and implementing that re-prompt mechanic.

For State D (spinning), the system detects it and would need to raise an attention flag, but `needs_guidance` is agent-raised in the current auth table — there's no system-raised `needs_guidance` defined yet.

The migration number needs to shift from 042 to 043, and the idle-rail false-positive fix is noted as a separate handoff that my design depends on as the backstop for the no-silent-halt invariant.

Looking at alignment: the status redesign already delivers most of what I was planning for Phase 3 — attention=`error` is non-terminal and non-releasing, `needs_guidance` exists for recoverable cases, and the plumbing for raising attention with reasons is there. Phase 2 (State D) now raises attention non-terminally, which is much safer. So the rebase changes my roadmap: Phase 1 needs reconciliation with my sweep changes (heartbeat, State B guard, loop removal) merging with the existing work.

The conflicts are substantial — WorkstreamLivenessSweep.ts, ProjectionSnapshotQuery.ts, Migrations.ts, and test files all have overlapping changes. Beyond textual conflicts, my code references `thread.status` and related fields that no longer exist in the new thread shell shape with planLane and attention, so this requires careful semantic adaptation. Given the complexity and full context, delegating the rebase to someone with a precise brief makes sense — the contract is clear: adapt Phase 1 liveness changes onto the new three-axis model.

But actually, the user's core ask is to rebase and confirm alignment, and a sub-thread doing conflict resolution mid-rebase in my working tree is risky and stateful. I'll do the rebase resolution myself since I have the full context, get the gates green, then report back on alignment.

content.text:
This is excellent alignment — and it changes my remaining roadmap substantially. Before I rebase, the key takeaways from their signed design:

- **My Phase 3 is largely *delivered* by this redesign.** Attention `error` is no longer terminal — it doesn't release dependents, doesn't revert the plan lane, and clears when the condition clears. The catastrophic harm of a false verdict (killing healthy work, releasing waiters) is *structurally gone*. Plus `needs_guidance` exists for recoverable cases, and there's a "no silent halt" invariant with the idle rail as backstop.
- **The "wake/surface without terminal kill" plumbing I worried about building already exists** as `thread.attention.raise { reason }`.
- **State B is now first-class** (`awaiting_input`/`awaiting_approval`, system-derived) and the new sweep already skips any thread with `attention.length > 0`.
- **State D (Phase 2) becomes much safer** — raising attention is non-terminal, so a false spin-flag just surfaces "needs a human," it doesn't kill.

content.text:
Clean integration — all gates green:
- **typecheck:** 0 errors
- **`vp check`:** 0 errors (13 pre-existing warnings)
- **Tests:** 107/107 across the four overlapping suites (`WorkstreamLivenessSweep`, `ProviderRuntimeIngestion`, `ProjectionSnapshotQuery`, `WorkstreamDispatcher`)

The rebase needed only three resolutions: renumbering my migration `042 → 043` (theirs took 042), the `Migrations.ts` registration, and the migration import. Everything else **auto-merged coherently** because the two efforts touched different regions of the same files — my loop-removal + heartbeat slotted in alongside their `attention.raise` + `planLane`/`attention` changes. Notably, my `markError` *automatically inherited* their change: it now raises **attention `error`** instead of setting a terminal status.

## Does it align with my expectations? Yes — strongly, and it does some of my work for me

The three-axis model is a better-realised version of exactly what we designed toward:

- **The "wake/surface without terminal kill" plumbing I was worried about building now exists** — `thread.attention.raise { reason }` is the notification surface, and `done` is the *sole* releaser of dependents. So a false liveness verdict can no longer kill healthy work or release waiters. **The core harm that started this whole investigation is now structurally impossible**, independent of detection quality.
- **State B (waiting for input) is first-class** (`awaiting_input`/`awaiting_approval`), and the sweep already skips any thread with a raised attention flag — my `hasPendingUserInput` guard is now belt-and-suspenders on top of that.
- **State D (Phase 2) just got much safer** — raising attention is non-terminal, so a false spin-flag merely surfaces "needs a human," it doesn't terminate anything.

## What this means for the roadmap

**Phase 3 (your "failed vs stalled" concern) is mostly *delivered* by the redesign.** A stall no longer terminally kills — it raises a non-terminal attention flag that clears when the condition clears, with the idle rail as an async backstop. What *remains* of Phase 3 is now optional polish, not harm-removal:
1. **Semantic accuracy:** a stall currently raises attention `error` (the redesign's bucket for "failed/stalled/looping"). We could instead raise `needs_guidance` so the board distinguishes "recoverable stall, needs a poke" from "genuinely failed."
2. **Auto-recovery:** auto-nudge/re-prompt a stalled child once before surfacing — the actual "just force the next turn" mechanic from your malformed-tool-call example.

So the open choices are now:
- **Phase 2 (State D):** still the highest-value-but-riskiest piece, now de-risked by non-terminal attention + the disable flag. Green light?
- **Phase 3 polish:** worth doing (semantic `needs_guidance` + auto-nudge), or leave stalls as attention-`error` for now since they no longer kill?
- **Shipping:** Phase 1 (rebased) is a clean, green, self-contained PR that stops the false kills *and* fixes the long-reasoning-looks-silent gap. Ship it now, or keep batching?

Nothing is pushed — the rebase is local on the feature branch. Your call on all three.

On 1. **Semantic accuracy:** - I agree
On 2. I think only order recovery aspect. Yes, but for just nudging it should probably attempt to read the pi jsonl file to understand what the error was that was thrown (or what happened).

Let's do phase 3 polish (implement code + review) then ship what we've done. *then* we can implement Phase 2

Is there some way that we can have State D easily disable-able if I just keep running into issues with it (this is prototype code, so it doesn't need to be prod ready.)

I'm working through the semantic distinction between a stall and an actual error—a stall should trigger a `needs_guidance` status to indicate it's recoverable, rather than treating it as a hard failure. For recovery attempts, I should check the pi jsonl file to understand what error was originally thrown or what state the process reached. nudge to be informed by the JSONL error context. So the design should be: when State C stall is detected, read the pi session JSONL to extract what error occurred, raise an attention signal with that error context included, and surface it to the parent — then the parent can decide whether to auto-nudge or intervene manually. This keeps the recovery informed rather than blind. So the user wants an informed auto-nudge: the system reads the JSONL to extract what error occurred, then re-prompts the child with that context so it can address the issue and continue. This is a turn-driving action that doesn't change status semantics—it just engages the child again through the existing wake/prompt mechanism. The question is whether the system should auto-nudge or wait for the parent to decide.

The answer is an escalation ladder: on first stall, read the JSONL, send an informed re-prompt to the child, and only raise `needs_guidance` if the stall persists after the nudge. This matches the user's intent—attempt recovery automatically, escalate to human only if recovery fails. For Phase 3, that means stall surfaces as `needs_guidance` (not `error`), and the liveness sweep reads the session file, extracts the error context, and dispatches a recovery prompt to the child. The challenge is whether this fits within scope—it requires reading a file and sending a turn command, which are both feasible, but it's getting into more complex orchestration.

edits.oldText:
**Scope boundary (important):** this work is **detection + response (wake/
surface)** only. It does **not** redefine status semantics. A separate framework
rearchitecture is making `error` a runtime-*derived* lane rather than an
agent-set one; we produce the signals that model needs and use the parent-wake
path for recoverable cases, but we do not change what any status *means*.
edits.newText:
**Scope boundary (important):** this work is **detection + response (wake/
surface)** only. It does **not** redefine status semantics.

**UPDATE (post-rebase, PR #26 landed):** the three-axis status model is now in
`main` (`.plans/workstream-state-model-design.md`, signed). Key consequences for
this work, already inherited by Phase 1 after rebase:
- The liveness response is now `thread.attention.raise { reason }` (one of
  `error | awaiting_approval | awaiting_input | awaiting_acceptance |
  needs_guidance`) — NOT `thread.status.set`. Attention is non-terminal: it does
  not release dependents (`done` is the sole releaser) and clears when the
  condition clears. **A false liveness verdict can therefore no longer kill
  healthy work** — the original harm is structurally gone.
- State B (waiting for input) is first-class: the sweep already skips any thread
  with `attention.length > 0`, and the classifier also guards on
  `hasPendingUserInput`/`hasPendingApprovals`.
- We still do not change what any status/attention reason *means*; we only choose
  which reason to raise. For meaningful auth/ownership questions (e.g. may the
  system auto-drive a recovery turn on a child?), consult the status-model
  author via `.plans/workstream-state-model-design.md`.
edits.oldText2:
### 3c. State C — the failed-vs-stalled fix

Your canonical example: an agent issues a malformed tool call, it errors, and
"nothing happens." The process is alive; it just needs a poke. Today C and A both
collapse to terminal `error`. In the end state C is a **recoverable stall**: wake
the parent (and/or auto-nudge once). The actual "stalled vs failed" *status
lane* is the status-redesign's job; we supply the detection and the recoverable
response, and do not set terminal status ourselves for C.
edits.newText2:
### 3c. State C — the failed-vs-stalled fix (Phase 3, now the next work)

Your canonical example: an agent issues a malformed tool call, it errors, and
"nothing happens." The process is alive; it just needs a poke. The fix has two
parts:

1. **Semantic accuracy.** A stall raises attention **`needs_guidance`**
   (recoverable — a human/poke is needed), NOT `error` (which the status model
   reserves for genuine failure). Dead (State A) keeps raising `error`.
2. **Informed auto-recovery (escalation ladder).** On the FIRST stall detection,
   read the child's **pi session JSONL** (path from pi's `sessionFile`) to
   extract what actually happened (the last thrown error / last event), then send
   ONE informed nudge to the child to recover (reuse the existing send-turn path,
   `ProviderCommandReactor`). Only if the child is STILL frozen on a later sweep
   (heartbeat unchanged since the nudge) escalate to attention `needs_guidance`,
   carrying the extracted context so the human sees *why*. A blind re-prompt is
   not enough — the nudge must be informed by the JSONL, because the error is in
   the transcript but the stalled child may be unaware of it.

Dedup like the circuit-breaker counter: nudge at most once per stall episode;
re-arm when the heartbeat advances. Do not raise `error` for a stall.

**Scope boundary (important):** this work is **detection + response (wake/
surface)** only. It does **not** redefine status semantics.

**UPDATE (post-rebase, PR #26 landed):** the three-axis status model is now in
`main` (`.plans/workstream-state-model-design.md`, signed). Key consequences for
this work, already inherited by Phase 1 after rebase:
- The liveness response is now `thread.attention.raise { reason }` (one of
  `error | awaiting_approval | awaiting_input | awaiting_acceptance |
  needs_guidance`) — NOT `thread.status.set`. Attention is non-terminal: it does
  not release dependents (`done` is the sole releaser) and clears when the
  condition clears. **A false liveness verdict can therefore no longer kill
  healthy work** — the original harm is structurally gone.
- State B (waiting for input) is first-class: the sweep already skips any thread
  with `attention.length > 0`, and the classifier also guards on
  `hasPendingUserInput`/`hasPendingApprovals`.
- We still do not change what any status/attention reason *means*; we only choose
  which reason to raise. For meaningful auth/ownership questions (e.g. may the
  system auto-drive a recovery turn on a child?), consult the status-model
  author via `.plans/workstream-state-model-design.md`.

edits.oldText:
### 3c. State C — the failed-vs-stalled fix

Your canonical example: an agent issues a malformed tool call, it errors, and
"nothing happens." The process is alive; it just needs a poke. Today C and A both
collapse to terminal `error`. In the end state C is a **recoverable stall**: wake
the parent (and/or auto-nudge once). The actual "stalled vs failed" *status
lane* is the status-redesign's job; we supply the detection and the recoverable
response, and do not set terminal status ourselves for C.
edits.newText:
### 3c. State C — the failed-vs-stalled fix (Phase 3, now the next work)

Your canonical example: an agent issues a malformed tool call, it errors, and
"nothing happens." The process is alive; it just needs a poke. The fix has two
parts:

1. **Semantic accuracy.** A stall raises attention **`needs_guidance`**
   (recoverable — a human/poke is needed), NOT `error` (which the status model
   reserves for genuine failure). Dead (State A) keeps raising `error`.
2. **Informed auto-recovery (escalation ladder).** On the FIRST stall detection,
   read the child's **pi session JSONL** (path from pi's `sessionFile`) to
   extract what actually happened (the last thrown error / last event), then send
   ONE informed nudge to the child to recover (reuse the existing send-turn path,
   `ProviderCommandReactor`). Only if the child is STILL frozen on a later sweep
   (heartbeat unchanged since the nudge) escalate to attention `needs_guidance`,
   carrying the extracted context so the human sees *why*. A blind re-prompt is
   not enough — the nudge must be informed by the JSONL, because the error is in
   the transcript but the stalled child may be unaware of it.

Dedup like the circuit-breaker counter: nudge at most once per stall episode;
re-arm when the heartbeat advances. Do not raise `error` for a stall.

**Disable requirement (explicit, prototype-grade):** State D is the highest
false-positive risk. It must be **trivially disableable by one edit** — a single
top-of-file boolean constant (e.g. `const ENABLE_STATE_D = true`) that
short-circuits the entire State-D branch when false. Flipping that one boolean
removes State D with zero other changes, and the branch must be self-contained so
it can equally be commented out or deleted in one place. No config plumbing or
feature-flag framework — this is prototype code; the one-liner is the point.

content.thinking:
The doc is ready, so I'm moving into Phase 3 and need to set up the coder and gated reviewer. For this phase, I'm refactoring how we handle stall verdicts—instead of raising them as errors, I'll treat them as `needs_guidance` signals, which means either modifying the error marking logic or splitting it so the verdict kind determines the severity level. I'm also sketching out an escalation ladder for informed auto-recovery that starts by reading the pi on first stall detection.

When a stall is detected, I'll extract the last error or event from the pi's JSONL session file and send a single informed nudge back through the existing send-turn path, tracking which heartbeat we nudged at per thread. If the heartbeat hasn't advanced by the next sweep, I escalate to `needs_guidance` with context; once the heartbeat moves again, I clear the nudge record. Dead verdicts still raise errors, but stalls don't. I need to be careful not to conflate status semantics and should check with the status-model owner about whether the system can auto-drive recovery turns.

The key contracts are the attention reasons enum, the pi's sessionFile path resolution server-side, the send-turn command dispatch through ProviderCommandReactor, and the WorkstreamLivenessSweep logic that classifies verdicts.

I'm leaving the exact JSONL parsing, nudge wording, sessionFile resolution, and escalation state structure to the coder, and I'll reference the liveness-detector and workstream-state-model design docs. Tests need to cover the stall-to-needs_guidance path, dead-to-error, and the nudge-once-then-escalate ladder. One risk: the auto-nudge is a new capability that might conflict with the authorization model around parent/human control of start/stop, so I'll flag that for escalation if it comes up. Also critical: a stalled turn has an open activeTurnId, so sending a recovery prompt needs to handle either steering mid-turn or interrupt-and-reprompt depending on pi's streaming behavior. The coder must account for that open-turn case.

Keeping this as one cohesive feature with one coder and one reviewer, where the coder marks status done to trigger the gated review.

content.text: Doc updated. Now spawning the Phase 3 coder and a reviewer gated on it. I'll tell the coder to finish at `done` (not `review`) so the reviewer auto-triggers — applying the gating lesson from last time.

arguments.purpose: Recoverable stalls stop being treated as failures: a stalled child is surfaced as "needs guidance" (not error) and the system first tries to unstick it with one nudge informed by reading the child's own session transcript, escalating to a human only if that fails.
arguments.title: Phase 3: recoverable-stall response (needs_guidance + informed auto-nudge)
arguments.brief:
You are a coder sub-thread in T3 Code (repo: pi-frontend, a Pi-first fork). CWD:
/home/Carl/.t3/cockpit/worktrees/pi-frontend/t3code-c61cd9a0. Fresh context — this brief is it.
Implement Phase 3 ("recoverable-stall response"). The earlier Phase 1 (runtime heartbeat, input-
aware sweep, loop detector removed) is already merged on this branch, rebased onto the new
three-axis status model.

## Read first (authoritative; consult on ambiguity)
- `.plans/liveness-detector-redesign.md` (signed, id 2d4d011f) — your manager. §3c is THIS task;
  §3a/§3b/§3f are context. For meaningful decisions the plan doesn't resolve, use `consult_manager`
  with author_doc=".plans/liveness-detector-redesign.md", author_role="architecture".
- `.plans/workstream-state-model-design.md` (signed) — the three-axis status model (plan lane /
  runtime / attention). Attention reasons and §8 authorisation matter here. If an auth question
  arises (see the risk gate below), consult THIS doc's author.
- Code: apps/server/src/orchestration/Layers/WorkstreamLivenessSweep.ts (the sweep + `classifyLiveness`
  + `markError`), apps/server/src/orchestration/Layers/ProviderCommandReactor.ts (the existing
  send-turn-to-a-thread path), apps/server/src/provider/Layers/Pi/RpcProcess.ts (pi's `sessionFile`),
  apps/server/src/provider/Services/ProviderSessionDirectory.ts (thread→session bindings),
  packages/contracts/src/orchestration.ts (the `thread.attention.raise { reason }` command + the
  attention-reason union).

## Contract to hit (pin these; everything else is your judgment)
1. **Semantic accuracy — stall raises `needs_guidance`, not `error`.** Today the sweep's `markError`
   raises attention `reason: "error"` for BOTH the dead and stalled verdicts. Split the response by
   verdict kind: `dead` (the circuit-breaker / process-gone case) keeps raising attention `error`;
   `stalled` must raise attention **`needs_guidance`** (recoverable — a human/poke is needed), never
   `error`. Keep the `workstream.liveness.<kind>` activity row.
2. **Informed auto-recovery (escalation ladder).** A stall must not jump straight to bothering a
   human. On the FIRST sweep that detects a stall for a thread:
   a. Locate and read the child's **pi session JSONL** (path via pi's `sessionFile` — resolve it for
      the thread through the session record / `ProviderSessionDirectory`; discover the exact route).
   b. Extract a concise account of what happened — the last thrown tool error / last meaningful
      event — enough to make a nudge informed (a blind re-prompt is explicitly NOT acceptable: the
      error is in the transcript but the stalled child may be unaware of it).
   c. Send ONE nudge to the child to recover, carrying that context (reuse the existing send-turn
      path — `ProviderCommandReactor` / the dispatcher's send mechanism; do not invent new transport).
      Note a stalled turn is typically still OPEN (`activeTurnId` set), so handle the open-turn case
      correctly (pi supports mid-turn steering, `streamingBehavior: "steer"`; or interrupt+reprompt —
      your call, justify it).
   d. Track per-thread nudge state (mirror the existing serial-safe `failureCounts` Map). Nudge **at
      most once per stall episode**. If a later sweep still sees the thread stalled (heartbeat
      unchanged since the nudge), THEN escalate → raise attention `needs_guidance` with the extracted
      context so the human sees *why*. Re-arm (clear the nudge record) when the heartbeat advances or
      the thread leaves the stalled condition.
3. **Do not change status semantics.** You only choose which attention reason to raise and drive a
   recovery turn; you do not redefine reasons, lanes, or the runtime axis.

## Risk gate (stop-and-consult, do not improvise)
The auto-nudge means the SYSTEM drives a recovery turn on a child. The status model's §8 says
start/stop authority is the parent orchestrator or a human. If wiring the nudge requires anything
that conflicts with that authorisation model, or you cannot send a turn without entangling status
semantics, STOP and `consult_manager` (author_doc=".plans/workstream-state-model-design.md") and/or
report back for guidance rather than forcing it.

- `vp run typecheck` and `vp check` must pass. Run `vp test` for the liveness + reactor suites.

  nudge-once-then-escalate ladder (nudge on first stall; escalate only if still frozen; re-arm on
  heartbeat advance). Keep tests deterministic; classifier/decision logic should stay pure where it
  can.
- Note the pre-existing unrelated failure `serverRuntimeStartup.test.ts:30` (default-model drift) —
  not yours; ignore it but confirm you didn't add new failures.
- Keep the diff minimal and prototype-clean (no back-compat shims). Report via workstream_report:
  what changed, how you resolve the JSONL path, the nudge transport you chose and why, the
  escalation/dedup state, any consult you ran, and gate/test results. Then **set your status to
  `done`** (a reviewer is gated on that). Do NOT open a PR.

   via `sessionFile`/session binding) and includes extracted error/context. A re-prompt that does
   NOT read the transcript is a must-fix. Check JSONL read failures are handled (missing/locked file
   must not crash the sweep).
3. **Escalation ladder is bounded and correct.** Nudge at most ONCE per stall episode (not every 60s
   sweep); escalate to `needs_guidance` only if still frozen after the nudge; re-arm when the
   heartbeat advances. Look hard for: a nudge loop (re-nudging forever), a stranded child (stalled
   but neither nudged nor escalated), and races in the per-thread state map. The dedup state must be
   serial-safe like the existing `failureCounts`.
4. **Open-turn handling.** A stalled turn is usually open (`activeTurnId` set); confirm the chosen
   transport (steer vs interrupt+reprompt) actually delivers to an open turn and is justified.
5. **Authorisation (hard gate).** The system driving a recovery turn must not violate the status
   model's §8 (start/stop = parent/human). If the coder proceeded past this without a consult and it
   looks like a violation, that's request-changes. Confirm no status-semantic redefinition.

   `serverRuntimeStartup.test.ts:30` model-drift failure is NOT in scope). Run the liveness + reactor
   test suites; confirm the new ladder tests are deterministic and actually assert the behaviour.

accept / request-changes verdict via workstream_report, then set status to `done`. Do not edit code

Both done, reviewer accepts. Let me read both full reports to validate the verdict before I accept and prepare the ship.

## Phase 3 — recoverable-stall response (State C) delivered

A stalled sub-thread (open turn, runtime heartbeat frozen past the window) is now **recovered, not killed**: the system sends one informed nudge to un-stick it, and only escalates to a human — as **`needs_guidance`**, never `error` — if it stays frozen.

### What changed
- **`apps/server/src/orchestration/stallContext.ts` (new)** — pure `extractStallContext` pulls the last meaningful event from the child's pi session JSONL (errored `toolResult` or last assistant message, whichever is last, so stale errors behind later progress aren't resurfaced), `renderStallContext`, and `readThreadStallContext` (the thin reader). It never fails the sweep (missing/unreadable file → null → uninformed-but-still-sent nudge).
- **`WorkstreamLivenessSweep.ts`** — split the old `markError`:
  - `dead` → `markDead` → attention **`error`** (unchanged).
  - `stalled` → escalation ladder. Verdict now carries `effectiveActivityMs` (the stall-episode key). Pure `decideStallAction`: **nudge** on the first sweep of an episode; **escalate** when the same episode is still frozen on a later sweep; **re-arm** to nudge when the heartbeat advances (new episode); **escalate** (not nudge) if there's no open turn. Serial-safe `stallNudges` Map mirrors `failureCounts` and stashes the extracted context so escalation reuses it without a second read.

### How the JSONL path is resolved
`resolveSessionFilePath(piSessionIdForThread(thread.id))` — the same deterministic-id → absolute-`.jsonl` resolution `consult_thread`/`workstream_ask` already use; scans the sessions root only.

### Nudge transport (no new transport)
A `thread.turn.start` (no `requireIdle`, no `setInProgress`) → ProviderCommandReactor → `providerService.sendTurn`. Because the stalled turn is open, **PiDriver auto-sends `streamingBehavior:"steer"`**, folding the nudge into the live agent loop rather than starting a fresh turn. `server:`-prefixed, **episode-keyed** command ids (idempotent within an episode, re-armable across episodes). The nudge message carries a control-plane marker (`[T3 Workstream control plane — automated notice, not from the user]`) + the extracted account + a recover-or-explain instruction.

### Authorisation (risk gate — resolved, not bypassed)
The status-model author session **was on disk** — I initially misread `consult_manager`'s "No session found" as "not on disk"; it was the cross-worktree id-scoping trap (author session lives under the `df4695a1` slug, I'm in `c61cd9a0`). Re-consulted via the **absolute path** and got a real ruling (**medium confidence**): §8's start/stop reservation targets agent/human actors; a `server:`-prefixed steer into an **already-open** turn that writes neither `in_progress` nor stored attention is, by the model's own definition, **not a §8 "start"** → sanctioned. Hard guardrail it added and I implemented: **the nudge fires only when `activeTurnId` is set** (else escalate) — a closed turn must never become a fresh start. Logged in `progress.md` with author + confidence.

### Gates
- `vp run typecheck` **PASS**; `vp check` **PASS** (0 errors; 13 pre-existing web-component warnings).
- New unit tests **PASS**: `stallContext.test.ts` (9) + extended `WorkstreamLivenessSweep.test.ts` (ladder, nudge-message, `effectiveActivityMs`) — 21 total.
- Pre-existing failures, **confirmed not mine by stashing my changes and re-running**: `ProviderCommandReactor.test.ts` ×2 (title-match poll timeouts) and the noted `serverRuntimeStartup.test.ts:30` default-model drift.

### For the reviewer / parent
- A full end-to-end run against a *real* stall wasn't exercised (needs a genuinely frozen child); validation is unit-level + real-JSONL-shape-derived fixtures + the typecheck/lint/test gates.
- The auth ruling is medium-confidence; the conservative guardrails (open-turn-only, no lane/attention mutation, server-prefixed) are what make it safe.
- No PR opened, per brief.

## Summary

A stalled sub-thread is now **recovered before a human is bothered**, and when a human *is* needed it's surfaced as **`needs_guidance`**, not `error`.

**Code:**
- New `apps/server/src/orchestration/stallContext.ts` — pure JSONL extraction of the last meaningful event (errored tool result or last assistant message) from the child's pi session transcript, plus the thin reader that resolves the deterministic session file.
- `WorkstreamLivenessSweep.ts` — split the old blunt `markError`: `dead` keeps raising attention `error`; `stalled` runs an escalation ladder (pure `decideStallAction`): one informed nudge first, escalate to `needs_guidance` only if still frozen

content.text:
## Phase 3 review — ACCEPT ✅

The recoverable-stall response (State C) is correct, bounded, and authorised. A stalled open-turn child now gets one informed steer to recover and escalates to `needs_guidance` (never `error`) only if still frozen. No request-changes items.

### Verified against the §3c contract and §8 authorisation
1. **Semantic split** ✓ — `classifyLiveness` returns only `dead`|`stalled`; `dead`→`markDead`→attention **`error`**, `stalled`→`escalateStall`→attention **`needs_guidance`**. No stall path can raise `error`. Activity row preserved on all three actions (`appendLivenessActivity` in markDead/nudge/escalate).
2. **Informed, not blind** ✓ — `readThreadStallContext` resolves the deterministic pi `.jsonl` via `resolveSessionFilePath(piSessionIdForThread(...))` and extracts the last meaningful event (errored toolResult or last assistant, whichever is later). Read failures are swallowed (`readFile(...).catch(()=>"")` → null → uninformed-but-sent nudge); the sweep cannot crash on a missing/locked file.
3. **Ladder bounded & correct** ✓ — `stallNudges` Map (plain serial-fiber map, mirrors `failureCounts`) keyed by thread, storing the `effectiveActivityMs` episode key + stashed context. Nudge fires once per episode; same key next sweep → escalate; escalate then `delete`s the entry and the raised `needs_guidance` makes subsequent sweeps skip via the `attention.length>0` guard → **no nudge loop**. `hasOpenTurn:false`→escalate, `verdict===null`/dead/terminal/no-session all `delete` the entry → **no stranded child**. Heartbeat advance changes the key → re-arms to nudge. Escalation reuses the stashed context (no second read).
4. **Open-turn transport** ✓ — nudge is a `thread.turn.start` (no `requireIdle`, no `setInProgress`). Confirmed in `PiDriver.sendTurn` (l.902–921): an active `session.activeTurnId` → `streamingBehavior:"steer"`, same turn id, no lifecycle re-emit. Folds into the live loop.
5. **Authorisation (hard gate)** ✓ — decider `thread.turn.start` writes `in_progress` only on `setInProgress===true` (dispatcher-only); the nudge omits it. It clears stored attention, but the sweep only acts on threads with `attention.length===0`, so that's a no-op. Open-turn-only guardrail enforced via `decideStallAction`. Author consult re-run via absolute path → medium-confidence sanction logged in progress.md. No status-semantic redefinition.
6. **Gates** — `vp run typecheck` **0 errors**; `vp check` **0 errors / 13 pre-existing mobile warnings** (out of scope). New tests **21/21 pass** (`stallContext.test.ts` 9 + extended sweep tests: ladder, nudge message, episode key) — all assert against **pure** functions (`decideStallAction`, `buildStallNudgeMessage`, `extractStallContext`), so deterministic. I independently confirmed the 2 `ProviderCommandReactor.test.ts` title-match poll-timeout failures are **pre-existing** by stashing the change and re-running on the clean tree (identical 2 failures).
7. **Prototype hygiene** ✓ — minimal diff (split `markError`→`markDead` + ladder; one new pure module), no back-compat shims, no over-engineering.

### Notes for the parent (non-blocking, nice-to-have)
- **`hasOpenTurn:false → escalate` is currently unreachable.** The classifier only emits `stalled` when `session.activeTurnId !== null`, the same signal the ladder reads, so the no-open-turn branch is belt-and-suspenders (the coder labels it as such). Harmless; flag only if someone later assumes it's live.
- **Narrow race (theoretical):** between the snapshot read and `sendTurn`, the driver turn could close, making the "steer" land as a fresh turn (new turnId, status→running). It still writes **no plan lane**, so it's not a §8 `in_progress` violation, and re-prompting is the intended recovery — but it momentarily exceeds the strict "steer-only" framing the auth argument leans on. Acceptable for prototype.
- **Auth ruling is medium-confidence** (author's own caveat); the conservative guardrails (open-turn-only, no lane/attention mutation, `server:`-prefixed) are what make it safe.
- **Not exercised end-to-end against a real frozen child** — validation is unit-level + real-JSONL-shaped fixtures + gates. A live smoke test on a genuine stall would be worth doing before merge if cheap.

**Verdict: accept.** No code edited, no PR opened (per brief).

Review complete — **ACCEPT**.

Phase 3's recoverable-stall response holds up against the §3c contract and the §8 authorisation gate. I verified, with file:line evidence (not just the report):

content.text:
**Shipped.** PR #27 is merged to `main` and the feature branch is deleted. Phases 1 + 3 are live.

## ⚠️ The one judgment call I want you to see (and can veto)

Phase 3 introduces a **new system behavior**: when a child stalls, the watchdog now *auto-steers its open turn* with an informed nudge. That's the system driving a turn on a child, which brushes against the status model's rule that start/stop authority belongs to the parent/human (§8).

The coder consulted the status-model's author, who ruled it **sanctioned at *medium* confidence**: a `server:`-prefixed steer into an *already-open* turn that writes neither `in_progress` nor any stored attention is, by the model's own definition, **not a "start"** — so it doesn't violate §8. The guardrails that make it safe: **open-turn-only** (a closed turn can never become a fresh start — it escalates instead), no plan-lane/attention writes, server-prefixed idempotent command. The reviewer independently confirmed all of this and accepted.

I shipped it because you'd approved shipping and it's guarded + reversible (prototype, one `git revert`). But it's *medium*, not high, confidence on a genuinely new capability — so if auto-steering children feels wrong to you, say so and I'll revert that piece (Phase 1 stands alone fine).

## What's now live
- **Phase 1:** runtime heartbeat (reasoning/token deltas included), input-aware sweep, loop detector retired, `loop→error` gone. A false verdict can no longer kill or release anything (inherited from the three-axis model).
- **Phase 3:** stalls are *recovered, not killed* — one informed steer (read from the child's JSONL), escalating to `needs_guidance` (never `error`) only if still frozen. Dead still raises `error`.

## Honest residuals
- **Not exercised end-to-end against a genuinely frozen child** — validation is unit tests + real-JSONL-shaped fixtures + green gates. A live stall is expensive to manufacture (needs a real frozen child + the 2m grace + 10m window), so I didn't. Flagging per the "verify against the real entrypoint" bar.
- **Pre-existing failures still red** (not introduced here, confirmed by stash-and-rerun): `serverRuntimeStartup.test.ts:30` (the model-string drift you said to park) and `ProviderCommandReactor.test.ts` ×2 (title-match poll timeouts). Worth a separate cleanup pass.
- A narrow theoretical race (turn closes between snapshot and steer) lands the nudge as a fresh turn — still writes no plan lane, so not a §8 violation; acceptable for prototype.

## Next
Per your sequencing, that leaves **Phase 2 (State D — "busy but not progressing")**, behind the one-line `ENABLE_STATE_D` kill switch. Want me to kick off the Phase 2 coder + reviewer now, or hold while you sit with the auto-steer behavior first?
Kick off phase 2, *then* help me understand *how* the watchdog now *auto-steers its open turn* with an informed nudge. I don't understand this. Walk me through with an example.

content.thinking:
I need to set up Phase 2 with the coder and reviewer working together on State D—the scenario where progress has stalled. Then I'll walk through a concrete example of how the watchdog mechanism kicks in during Phase 3 to auto-steer an open turn with a nudge, but I'm still working out the detection criteria for that stuck state.

For the coder, I'll pull thread checkpoint context and full diff context to establish what's actually happening, optionally cross-checking with recent tool activity signals from Phase 1. The key question is what attention reason the system should raise when it detects possible spinning—whether that's `error` (system-detected looping) or `needs_guidance` (recoverable, needs human judgment). The auth model treats `needs_guidance` as agent-raised, but State D is system-detected, so I need to clarify whether the system can raise `needs_guidance` or if it should use `error` instead. The detection logic hinges on two conditions: the heartbeat advancing (showing active work) while the checkpoint diff stays flat or oscillates, with a generous ~10-minute window to avoid false positives. I'll make the feature toggleable via a single constant and validate against real state data, constructing a test case where work is happening but producing no progress. The response should surface an advisory to the parent without terminal semantics, though I need to consult with the status-model author on whether to use `needs_guidance` or `error` for the attention reason, since Phase 3 moved stall semantics away from error toward recoverability.

The tricky part is detecting "diff unchanged over the window"—getFullThreadDiffContext gives me a diff from checkpoint 0 to a turn count, but I need to compare diffs at two points in time. I'm thinking the cleanest approach is to store a rolling work-product fingerprint (hash) per thread per sweep alongside the heartbeat state, similar to how stallNudges tracks nudge counts, then flag when that fingerprint hasn't changed for the full window while the heartbeat keeps advancing. This avoids recomputing expensive diffs every 60 seconds and keeps the state lightweight.

Before I write the brief and spawn the coder, I need to nail down the State D attention-reason contract—I don't want the coder guessing wrong. I'm pinning it as: State D surfaces a non-terminal advisory to the parent without touching plan lane or terminal status. The exact attention reason for a system-detected possible-spin is owned by the status model, so I need to consult the status-model author before finalizing. My instinct is to avoid `error` (too strong for a heuristic) and use a warning-tone activity with the softest attention reason they'll sanction for system use.

Also clarifying: raising attention IS the notification surface in the new model, so State D waking the parent is automatic. I'll apply the same "at most once per episode" dedup as the stall ladder to prevent loops.

Now I'm ready to write the brief and spawn the coder and reviewer, then walk through the auto-steer mechanism with an example.

The sweep runs every 60 seconds and checks heartbeats on all running sub-threads. If a child thread gets wedged mid-turn—say it made a malformed edit call that errored and the agent loop froze—the heartbeat stays frozen at that error time. After 10+ minutes past the startup grace period, the sweep marks it stalled. Instead of killing it, the sweep reads the thread's session file, extracts the last meaningful event (the actual error), and injects a nudge message directly into the live agent loop as a "steer." The agent receives the error context, hopefully recovers, and resumes—heartbeat advances and the thread unstalls.

If the nudge doesn't work and the heartbeat is still frozen at the next sweep, the system escalates to human attention with the extracted error context so you see exactly what the child is stuck on. The nudge only fires once per stall episode to avoid spam, and it only works if the turn is still open—if the turn had already closed, a steer would become a fresh turn, which isn't allowed, so it escalates instead. Now I'm ready to walk through a concrete timeline example and then write the Phase 2 coder brief.

content.text: Kicking off Phase 2 now, then I'll walk you through the auto-steer with a concrete example.

arguments.purpose: Catch the genuinely-stuck case the cheap rails can't: a child that's busy (heartbeat ticking) but making no real progress (work product flat) gets surfaced to its parent as a non-terminal advisory — behind a one-line kill switch so it can be disabled instantly if it proves noisy.
arguments.title: Phase 2: State D — progress-based spin detection (behind kill switch)
arguments.brief:
You are a coder sub-thread in T3 Code (repo: pi-frontend, a Pi-first fork). CWD:
/home/Carl/.t3/cockpit/worktrees/pi-frontend/t3code-c61cd9a0. Fresh context — this brief is it.
Implement Phase 2 ("State D — possibly spinning"). Phases 1 and 3 are already merged on `main`
(runtime heartbeat, input-aware sweep, loop detector removed, recoverable-stall ladder). Start from
the current `main` after `git fetch origin main` — base your work on it.

## Read first (authoritative; consult on ambiguity)
- `.plans/liveness-detector-redesign.md` (signed, id 2d4d011f) — your manager. §3d is THIS task;
  §3a/§3b/§3c are context now-implemented. For meaningful decisions the plan doesn't resolve, use
  `consult_manager` (author_doc=".plans/liveness-detector-redesign.md", author_role="architecture").
- `.plans/workstream-state-model-design.md` (signed) — the three-axis status model. The attention
  vocabulary is THEIRS; see the auth question below and consult that doc's author for it.
- Code: apps/server/src/orchestration/Layers/WorkstreamLivenessSweep.ts (the sweep, `classifyLiveness`,
  the serial-safe per-thread state maps `failureCounts`/`stallNudges` — mirror that pattern),
  apps/server/src/orchestration/Services/ProjectionSnapshotQuery.ts (it RETAINS
  `getRecentToolActivityByThreadId` + `ProjectionToolActivitySignal` from Phase 1 for optional
  identity corroboration), the checkpoint/diff substrate `getThreadCheckpointContext` /
  `getFullThreadDiffContext` (search checkpointing/ + ProjectionSnapshotQuery for how diffs are
  obtained for a thread).

## What State D is (and is NOT)
State D fires when a child is **busy but not progressing**: the runtime heartbeat IS advancing (it's
actively emitting events) BUT the **work product is flat** across the window (the coder's cumulative
checkpoint diff is unchanged / oscillating). This is the ONLY way to tell "slow real work" (diff
growing) from "spinning" (diff flat) — do NOT reintroduce tool-call-repetition as the signal (that
was the retired loop detector). The confirmed false-positive shape (several distinct edits to one
file) MUST NOT fire State D, because each edit grows the diff.

## Contract to hit (pin these; the rest is your judgment)
1. **Kill switch (prototype-grade, mandatory).** A single top-of-file boolean
   `const ENABLE_STATE_D = true` (or equivalent one-liner) that short-circuits the ENTIRE State-D
   branch when false — flipping it removes State D with zero other edits. Keep the whole branch
   self-contained so it can also be commented out / deleted in one place. No config plumbing.
2. **Detection = progress, corroborated.** Trigger only when BOTH: (a) heartbeat advanced over the
   window (genuinely busy, not stalled — State C already owns frozen-heartbeat), AND (b) the work
   product has not changed across a generous `noProgressWindowMs` (start ~10m; document it as a tunable
   assumption). Optionally corroborate with repeated identical tool *inputs* via a hash of
   `rawInput` (the retained signal), but progress-flat is the primary gate.
   **Performance matters (core project priority):** do NOT recompute a full diff every 60s sweep for
   every busy thread. Use a cheap work-product *fingerprint* (e.g. checkpoint count, changed-file
   list + sizes, or a cheap digest) stored per-thread across sweeps (mirror `stallNudges`), and flag
   only when the fingerprint is unchanged for the window while the heartbeat advances.
3. **Response = non-terminal advisory, once per episode.** Surface to the parent (the attention axis
   is the notification surface) with EVIDENCE (busy N min, work product unchanged, optionally the
   repeated input). It MUST NOT set any plan lane or terminal status, MUST NOT kill the thread, and
   must fire at most once per episode (dedup like the stall ladder; re-arm when the fingerprint
   advances). No nudge loop, no stranded thread.
4. **AUTH QUESTION — resolve, don't guess.** Which attention reason a SYSTEM-detected possible-spin
   should raise is a contract owned by the status model. Its auth table assigns `needs_guidance` to
   *agents* and `error` to *system* (and lists "looping" under system `error`). `error` is likely too
   strong for a heuristic advisory; `needs_guidance` is semantically right but agent-assigned. Before
   finalizing, `consult_manager` (author_doc=".plans/workstream-state-model-design.md") for the right
   reason for a system advisory, and implement what the author sanctions (record the consult +
   confidence in progress.md). Do not redefine any reason yourself.

## Verify before reporting
- `vp run typecheck` and `vp check` must pass. Run `vp test` for the liveness suite.
- Make the decision logic a PURE function (like `decideStallAction`) and unit-test it: spinning
  (busy + flat fingerprint past window) → advisory once; growing diff → never; frozen heartbeat →
  NOT State D (that's State C); `ENABLE_STATE_D=false` → never fires. Validate against recorded
  evidence `~/.t3/cockpit/userdata/state.sqlite` (python3; `sqlite3` CLI absent): thread `48d7345f`
  (the same-file-edit run) must NOT produce a State-D advisory.
- Note pre-existing unrelated failures (`serverRuntimeStartup.test.ts:30`, `ProviderCommandReactor`
  ×2) — confirm you didn't add new ones.
- Keep the diff minimal, prototype-clean (no back-compat shims). Report via workstream_report: the
  fingerprint you chose + why (esp. performance), the detection/dedup state, the auth consult result,
  gate/test results. Then **set your status to `done`** (a reviewer is gated on it). Do NOT open a PR.

details.title: Review: Phase 2 State D spin detection

content.thinking: I've got the coder and reviewer running now. Let me walk through how the auto-steer mechanism actually works with a concrete example and timeline to make it clear.

content.text:
Phase 2 is underway — coder `c779d738`, reviewer `dac50f6b` gated behind it.

Now, the auto-steer walkthrough.

## What "auto-steer the open turn with an informed nudge" actually means

Three concepts first, then a worked example.

**1. "Open turn."** When a child agent is working, it's running a *turn* — one prompt→response cycle that can span many internal model rounds and tool calls. While that turn is in flight, the thread has an `activeTurnId` set. We call that an **open turn**. (When the agent finishes and goes idle, the turn closes — `activeTurnId` becomes null.)

**2. "Steer."** The runtime supports injecting a message *into a turn that's already running* — exactly what you do in the TUI when you type something mid-response to redirect the agent without stopping it. Pi calls this `streamingBehavior: "steer"`. It's different from starting a *fresh* turn: the message folds into the live agent loop. The key behavior: when we send a turn to a child whose turn is **already open**, PiDriver automatically routes it as a steer rather than a new turn.

**3. "Informed nudge."** Rather than a generic "are you stuck?", the message is built from reading the child's *own session transcript* (its pi `.jsonl` file) to find the last meaningful thing that happened — usually the error that wedged it. So the nudge tells the child *what* went wrong.

## Worked example

A coder child `C` is editing a file. Walk the timeline:

- **10:00:00** — `C` issues an `edit` tool call, but the `oldText` doesn't match anything in the file. The edit **fails** with an error. The agent loop doesn't handle it well and just… stops emitting events. The turn is still **open** (`activeTurnId` still set) — nothing closed it. `C` is now silently wedged.
- The **heartbeat** (the "last runtime activity" timestamp from Phase 1, which ticks on *any* event — tokens, reasoning, tools) is frozen at **10:00:00**, because no events are flowing.
- **10:01–10:11** — the background sweep runs every 60s. Each time it computes "time since last heartbeat." It's under the 10-minute stall window, so nothing happens. (Also past the 2-minute startup grace, so a slow first call isn't mistaken for this.)
- **10:12:00** — the sweep sees the heartbeat has been frozen for 12 minutes during an open turn → verdict: **stalled**.

Here's the new Phase 3 behavior instead of killing `C`:

1. **Resolve `C`'s transcript.** From `C`'s thread id it derives the deterministic pi session id, which maps to an absolute `.jsonl` path on disk.
2. **Read it, extract the cause.** It scans the JSONL for the last meaningful event and finds the errored tool result: *"edit failed: oldText not found in file X."* (If the file is missing or unreadable, it doesn't crash — it just sends an uninformed nudge.)
3. **Build the informed nudge:**
   > `[T3 Workstream control plane — automated notice, not from the user]`
   > Your turn appears stalled. The last event was: *edit failed — oldText not found in file X*. Please recover or explain what's blocking you.
4. **Send it as a steer.** Because `C`'s turn is **still open**, PiDriver injects this as `streamingBehavior: "steer"` into the live loop — same turn id, no new turn started. This is the "auto-steer its open turn" bit: the system is talking to `C` mid-turn, the way you would.
5. **It fires exactly once** for this stall episode (tracked in a per-thread map, just like the failure counter), so the system never spams.

Then one of two things happens:

- **The nudge works.** `C` receives the steer, sees the error, fixes its `oldText`, continues. New events flow → the heartbeat advances → the next sweep sees `C` is healthy again → the nudge state re-arms. **No human was ever involved.** This is the "recover, don't kill" win for your malformed-tool-call case.
- **The nudge doesn't work.** At **10:13:00** the sweep sees the heartbeat is *still* frozen (unchanged since before the nudge). Now it **escalates**: raises attention **`needs_guidance`** on `C`, carrying that extracted error context — so *you* (or the parent) get "child C is stuck on: *edit failed, oldText not found*; auto-nudge didn't help." That's the human handoff, and notably it's `needs_guidance` (recoverable, "needs a poke") — **not** `error` (genuine failure).

## The one safety rule that makes this legitimate

The steer is only sent **while the turn is open**. If the turn had already *closed* (agent went idle), sending a turn would start a brand-new turn — i.e. a "start," which the status model reserves for the parent/human (§8). So in the closed-turn case the system **doesn't** nudge; it escalates straight to `needs_guidance`. That open-turn-only guardrail — plus the fact that the steer writes no plan-lane and no stored status — is exactly why the status-model author sanctioned it (at medium confidence). It's "interject into a conversation that's already happening," never "start a new one."

Does that clear it up? If the medium-confidence auth basis still sits uneasily with you, the cleanest conservative alternative is to drop the auto-steer entirely and have a stall escalate *straight* to `needs_guidance` (let the parent/human do any re-prompt) — that removes the new system capability while keeping the failed-vs-stalled fix. Say the word and I'll make that change.

An implementation agent is consulting you about a decision it cannot resolve from the implementation plan and the codebase alone.
Use ONLY the inherited snapshot conversation (and read-only file inspection if absolutely needed) to answer.
Return the structured answer shape exactly as defined in your system prompt. Do not add preamble.

## Author lane
You authored .plans/liveness-detector-redesign.md as role=architecture. Stay strictly within that lane.

## Observation
Evidence from state.sqlite (thread 48d7345f, the same-file-edit false positive) shows checkpoints are written to projection_turns only at TURN END: that 4-min run with 8 distinct edits produced exactly 1 checkpoint row, captured at the final timestamp. Sub-threads run a single kickoff turn, so the checkpoint diff is FLAT for the entire working turn — it only materialises after the turn completes. The per-edit work product DOES live in the projection though: each tool.completed row carries data.details.diff/patch and data.rawInput, and these rows accumulate within the turn. getRecentToolActivityByThreadId already recovers each call's discriminating content (diff/path/command).

## Decision being considered
For State D's cheap work-product fingerprint, should within-turn tool-activity content (a digest over recent tool.completed diffs/inputs) be the PRIMARY progress signal, with checkpoint count/diff as a secondary/cross-turn corroborator — inverting §3d's stated 'checkpoint diff primary, input-hash optional corroboration' ordering?

## Options
1. Invert: make a digest of recent tool-activity content (diff/patch/rawInput) the primary within-turn progress signal, OR-combined with checkpoint progress (latest checkpoint_turn_count + summed additions/deletions) so EITHER advancing re-arms; flag only when the combined digest is flat across noProgressWindowMs while the heartbeat advances.
2. Keep checkpoint diff as the sole/primary signal as literally written in §3d (would mean State D essentially never distinguishes legit vs spinning within a single sub-thread turn, since checkpoints are flat until turn end — i.e. it would false-positive on any long single-turn coder).
3. Escalate to the user.

## Worker's recommendation
Invert per option 1: tool-activity-content digest is the only signal that actually grows with distinct within-turn edits, which is exactly the false-positive shape §3d must not fire on; checkpoints remain a useful OR-corroborator for cross-turn progress. This keeps progress-not-repetition as the principle (content changing = progress) rather than reverting to bare repeat-count.

## Blast radius if the recommendation is wrong
The fingerprint choice determines State D's entire false-positive profile. Wrong choice (checkpoint-only) silently reintroduces the exact false positive being fixed for any >10min single-turn coder; it's behind a kill switch but would make State D useless-or-harmful. Reversing later means re-deriving the signal and re-validating against recorded evidence.

content.thinking:
I'm reconsidering whether to flip the priority in §3d—making tool activity within a turn the primary progress signal instead of checkpoints, since checkpoints stay flat until the turn completes and sub-threads only run one turn. Let me check what the redesign doc actually says to see if this inversion aligns with the original intent.

The core principle throughout is "judge progress, not repetition"—content changing means progress. The whole point of State D is to avoid false positives on distinct edits to a single file. I also baked in a performance concern: don't recompute a full diff every 60 seconds, use a cheap fingerprint instead. The confirmed false-positive case shows that distinct edits to one file must not trigger State D.

The worker's observation is spot-on: since checkpoints only materialize at turn end and sub-threads run a single turn, the checkpoint diff stays flat the entire working turn—so using it as primary would false-positive on any long single-turn coder session, which directly contradicts §3d's spirit. But the within-turn tool activity (each completed tool carries diff/patch data and rawInput) actually grows with distinct edits, making it the real signal of within-turn progress. So inverting to make tool-activity content primary with checkpoint as a corroborator is fully consistent with the doc's intent.

The key guardrail is that this uses a digest over actual diff/patch content, not a bare repeat-count of tool calls—that's what keeps it aligned with "content changing = progress" and prevents reviving the old loop detector. This is squarely in my lane since it's about which signal State D uses, and I authored §3d. The snapshot states checkpoint-primary, but it also repeatedly emphasizes the overriding principle: don't false-fire on distinct within-turn edits. The worker surfaced a fact that means the literal ordering would violate the doc's own primary goal, so the intent trumps the literal substrate choice. I should flag that the digest needs to cover the actual diff/patch content and rawInput, not just the display summary. The content-digest approach correctly distinguishes genuine progress (edits that change the digest) from repetitive spinning (byte-identical re-emissions with flat digests), which is exactly what "progress not repetition" demands. This is fundamentally an implementation choice about which signal to prioritize—the architectural principle of progress-based detection remains intact, so I'll frame this as implementation-level rather than a structural shift, even though it does invert the documented ordering in the design doc.

content.text:
## Answer
Yes — invert per Option 1: a digest over within-turn tool-activity *content* (the per-edit diff/patch and `rawInput`) should be State D's primary progress signal, OR-combined with checkpoint progress as a cross-turn corroborator. This realises §3d's actual intent; the literal "checkpoint primary" wording was written without knowing checkpoints only materialise at turn end.

## Confidence
high

## Evidence
- §3d's overriding requirement (which I authored) is *progress, not repetition*: State D "must not fire" on the confirmed false-positive shape — several distinct edits to one file — because "each edit grows the diff." A flat-until-turn-end checkpoint signal would fire on exactly that shape for any long single-turn coder, directly violating the doc's primary goal.
- §3d already names the input/content path as a legitimate corroborator ("a hash of the raw tool input, `data.rawInput`") and explicitly warns it must be the real content, "NOT the display string."
- The Phase 2 brief in the snapshot demanded a "cheap work-product fingerprint" and forbade reviving tool-call-*repetition* as the signal; a content digest that changes when edits change is progress-detection, not repeat-counting.

## Reasoning
The snapshot says checkpoint-diff is primary (literal §3d) but says far more loudly that State D must never false-positive on real within-turn progress. The worker has surfaced a fact — checkpoints are flat until turn end, sub-threads run one turn — that makes the literal ordering *contradict* the intent. When a documented substrate choice defeats the documented goal, the goal wins; the content digest is the only signal that actually grows with within-turn work. The one guardrail I'm inferring (not merely restating): digest the *content* (`data.details.diff/patch`, `rawInput`), never the presentation summary, or you re-collapse into the retired loop detector's failure mode.

## Scope of impact
implementation only

## Recommended action for the worker
Proceed with Option 1 — content-digest primary, checkpoint as an OR-corroborator (either advancing re-arms; flag only when the combined digest is flat across `noProgressWindowMs` while the heartbeat advances). Digest actual diff/patch/`rawInput` content, not display strings. Leave a one-line code comment that this inverts §3d's literal ordering and why (checkpoints materialise only at turn end), so the doc and code don't appear to disagree silently.

## When to escalate to the user instead
If making the content digest primary would require treating byte-identical *repeated* edits as progress (i.e. you find you can't distinguish "new content" from "same content re-emitted" cheaply) — that would risk under-detecting genuine spin and re-opens a real design question for the user, not just an implementation call.
